comment_text.sn1.5:1.1: The redactors show their playful side here, placing this “riddle of fives” as the fifth sutta of the Saṁyutta. | The enigmatic repetition of “five” is reminiscent of Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.4.17: “Where the five groups of five and space are established, I regard that Self as the immortal Brahman, knowing which I am immortal.” Or perhaps it is a reference to the five great sacrifices, to sages, gods, ghosts, men and ancestors (Manu 4.21).
comment_text.sn1.5:3.1: According to the commentary, the five lower fetters are cut, the five higher fetters are dropped, and the five faculties are developed.
comment_text.sn1.5:3.3: The “five chains” are not defined in the Suttas, but Vb 17:238.1 identifies them as greed, hate, delusion, conceit, and views.

root_text.sn1.5:1.2: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitā kho sā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn1.5:1.2: Standing to one side, that deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.5:2.3: When a mendicant slips how many chains
translation_text.sn1.5:2.4: are they said to have crossed the flood?”
translation_text.sn1.5:3.3: When a mendicant slips five chains
translation_text.sn1.5:3.4: they’re said to have crossed the flood.”
